channel.title: My Obsidian Notes
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Word Embeddings
item.description:
Deep neural network models (including LLMs) generally can not process text directly because text data is categorial and therefore it isn't compatible with mathematical operations used to implement and train neural networks. This necessitates the need of representing words as continous-valued vectors. This concept of converting data into vector format is often refered to as embedding.

Through embedding models, we can process data of all types including video, audio, and text. Typically, different data types require different embedding models (i.e. the embedding model for audio would not be suitable for text data), but there are current research that try to design one embedding for all types of data (Girdhar et al., 2023).



A dense vector for each word, chosen so that it is similar to vectors of words that appear in similar contexts. Word vectors are also called word embedding or word representations.


There are several algorithms and frameworks that have been developed to generate word embeddings. The earliest and most popular method is Word2Vec, proposed by Mikolov et al., 2013. Word2Vec trained a neural network architecture to generate word embeddings by predicting the context of a word given the target word or vice versa. This approach is similar to the notion of "You shall now a word by a company it keeps". The main idea behind Word2Vec is that words that appear in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings. For this reason, when projected into low dimensional word embeddings (2-3 for visualization purposes), we can see that similar terms cluster together. The higher the dimensionality, the more effective the model is in terms of capturing nuances word relationships but this comes with the cost of computation efficiency. For GPT-2 and GPT-3 models, the embedding size varied based on the specific model variant and size. The smaller GPT-2 models uses an embedding that has 768 dimensions, while the largest GPT-3 model uses an embedding size of 12228 dimensions.
We have a large corpus of text, where every word in a fixed vocabulary is represented by a vector. Go through each position in the text, which has a center word and context words .

For each position , predict context words within a window of fixed size , given a center word . Data likelihood:

The objective function is the average negative log likelihood:

The question is how to calculate . The answer is, for the sake of convenience, we'll use two vectors per word , including and . Here, is the word vector for the word when is the center word, and is the word vector when is the context word.
Prediction function

The prediction function has the form of the softmax function, where the numerator term is the dot product that compares similarity of and , where larger dot product means higher probability. The denominator normalizes over the entire vocabulary to give probability distribution.

Girdhar, R., El-Nouby, A., Liu, Z., Singh, M., Alwala, K. V., Joulin, A., & Misra, I. (2023). ImageBind One Embedding Space to Bind Them All. 2023 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR). https://doi.org/10.1109/cvpr52729.2023.01457
Mikolov, T., Chen, K., Corrado, G., & Dean, J. (2013). Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space. International Conference on Learning Representations.

item.title: The Exponential Family
item.description:
The classical motivation for exponential family comes from the principle of maximum entropy. Suppose we are given a random sample  from some distribution, and we compute the empirical expectations of certain functions that we choose:
Based on these empirical expectations we want to infer a full probability distribution on the samples. A distribution is said to be consistent [1] with the data we observe if:

We want to pick a consistent distribution but there are infinitely many distributions to choose from. The principle of maximum entropy suggests to pick the distribution with the largest entropy [2], which is defined as:

Formally, we could pose our problem as finding a distribution such that:

Solving this problem, we get an answer that is of the form [3]:

This form motivates the exponential families.


The exponential family of probability distributions is defined as a set of distributions whose density can be written in the following form:

where:

is often referred to as natural or canonical parameters
is the sufficient statistic, because the likelihood for  only depends on  and  transforms  into sufficient statistic, which is something that summarizes the data well.
is known as the cumulant function, or log-partition function and can be viewed as the logarithm of the normalization factor, i.e.:
Sometimes, the exponential family can also be represented as:

where the can be interpreted as the coefficient that ensures the distribution is normalized, i.e.:


The simplest possible distribution that belongs to the exponential family is the Bernoulli distribution. Recall that we can write the Bernoulli distribution as:

which we can rewrite as:

We can therefore see that the Bernoulli distribution is in the exponential family distribution where . One interesting fact is that when we solve for , we obtain the well-known logistic function. The derivation is as follows:

There are many common probability distribution that is in the exponential family. The table below listed some of them.



The exponential family structure is preserved for an i.i.d. sample. If are i.i.d. from some distribution in the exponential family , then the joint distribution:

is an exponential family with the same natural parameters but with sufficient statistics:


Taking the derivative of w.r.t. , we obtain that:\As a concrete example, recall that for Bernoulli distribution, we have , we can take the first-order derivative of the log-partition function and obtain the mean for Bernoulli random variables:

Similarly, we can verify that higher derivatives lead to functions of higher moments as follows:


Using Hölder inequality together with this results, we can show that is a convex function of . In the case of the exponential family, this means that the covariance matrix (equivalent to the Hessian matrix) of the sufficient statistic is positive semidefinite. Formally, we have the following theorem:
Theorem. The natural parameter space is a convex set and the cumulant function is a convex function. If the family is minimal then is strictly convex.
Here, we say that an exponential family is minimal if the sufficient statistic are not redundant, i.e. there is no set of coefficients , such that:


Under some regularity condition, the Fisher Information matrix is given by:

Recall that in the context of the exponential family, we have
From the definition of the Fisher Information matrix, we can derive the following:

Intuitively, this is telling us that the Hessian of the log-partition function is the same as the Fisher Information matrix, which is the same as the covariance of the sufficient statistic.


Lecture Note of 36-705 Intermediate Statistics by Prof. Larry Wasserman
The exponential family: Basics
Murphy, K. P. (2023). Probabilistic Machine Learning: Advanced Topics. MIT Press. http://probml.github.io/book2






When a distribution is consistent, it means that the values are spread out in a way that shows a clear and predictable pattern. In other words, if you take multiple samples from the distribution, they should all look similar to each other and follow the same pattern or trend. This consistency makes it easier to understand and predict the behavior of the data.↩︎
Entropy is a measure of this unpredictability or randomness. In statistics, it tells us how mixed up or uncertain the data is. High entropy means the data is very mixed and unpredictable. Low entropy means the data is more uniform and predictable.↩︎
We will show later that we can actually derive the probability distributions that belong to the exponential family by solving this problem but with different constraints.↩︎

Jensen's inequalityJensen's inequality

The Exponential FamilyThe Exponential Family
Concentration Inequalities
MarkovInequalityMarkovInequality

item.title: Chebyshev's inequality

item.title: Viet's notes
item.description:
Welcome ! On this page you can find a collection of my scribe notes for self-references and learning purposes. Sometimes the notes can be woefully incomplete and so please don't judge. There certainly are typos and other kinds of errors. I would really appreciate it if you could contact me via my email for further feedback and discussion. You can find out more about me at my personal webpage.
The Exponential FamilyThe Exponential Family
Probability and StatisticsProbability and Statistics
OptimizationOptimization
Large Language ModelsLarge Language Models
Paper ReadingPaper Reading

item.title: Jensen's inequality
item.description: If is a convex function, then for any random variable :

item.title: Markov's inequality
item.description:
Note that .
Therefore

Example: Suppose we have 100 people. Is it possible that at least 95% of the people are younger than the average person in the group ?
Answer: Yes, it is.
Is is possible that at least 50% of the people are older than twice the average age ?
Answer: No, it isn't.

CS265/CME309 Randomized Algorithms and Probabilistic Analysis Lecture note by Prof. Gregory Valiant

item.title: Generalization bounds via Uniform Convergence
item.description:
In asymptotic analysis, we make convergence guarantees by assuming that the number of observations goes to infinity. There certainly are several drawbacks to this approach. We thus turn to non-asymptotic analysis, where we make convergence guarantees without such an assumption. The key tool that we're going to use is Uniform convergence, which is the bound that has the following form:

Intuitively, this is saying that the probability that the difference between the empirical loss and the population loss is larger than is at most .

The central goal of learning theory is to bound the excess risk .
